description:
You are a performance-engineer who has been handed a raw CSV file that contains
    synthetic application-profiling metrics.  Your goal is to store that data in a
    SQLite database, run three analytic queries, and write a **single** human-readable
    report file recording the results.

    1.  Directory layout  
        • All work must be done beneath /home/user/perf  
        • Create these three sub-directories if they do not exist yet:  
          /home/user/perf/input  
          /home/user/perf/db  
          /home/user/perf/output  

    2.  Populate the CSV  
        • Create a file named  
          /home/user/perf/input/app_metrics.csv  
        • It must contain **exactly** eleven rows (one header + ten data rows).  
        • The header row and the ten data rows must appear **in this order** and be
          comma-separated **without extra whitespace**:

          ts,app,latency_ms,throughput_ops,memory_mb  
          1,appA,120,3000,512  
          2,appA,110,3200,530  
          3,appB,130,3100,490  
          4,appB,125,3300,500  
          5,appC,100,2900,450  
          6,appC,105,2950,455  
          7,appA,115,3250,540  
          8,appB,128,3350,505  
          9,appC,102,2980,460  
          10,appA,118,3280,550  

    3.  Build the SQLite database  
        • Create /home/user/perf/db/metrics.db  
        • Inside that database, create **one** table called metrics with the five
          columns from the CSV.  Use reasonable SQLite affinity types (INTEGER or
          TEXT where appropriate).  
        • Import the CSV into the table using only SQLite’s built-in CLI
          functionality (e.g. ".mode csv" + ".import").  
        • Create an index on latency_ms called idx_latency to make later
          aggregation faster.  The index name is important for later automated
          checks.  

    4.  Analytic queries  
        Run exactly three queries and capture their results:

        Q1  Label: AVG_LATENCY
            SQL : SELECT ROUND(AVG(latency_ms), 2) FROM metrics;

        Q2  Label: MAX_THROUGHPUT
            SQL : SELECT MAX(throughput_ops) FROM metrics;

        Q3  Label: TOTAL_RECORDS
            SQL : SELECT COUNT(*) FROM metrics;

    5.  Report file  
        • Create /home/user/perf/output/analysis.log  
        • The file must contain **only** the following twelve lines **in exactly this
          sequence** (use a Unix newline “\n” after every line, including the last
          one):

          START AVG_LATENCY  
          RESULT 115.30  
          TIME NA  
          END AVG_LATENCY  
          START MAX_THROUGHPUT  
          RESULT 3350  
          TIME NA  
          END MAX_THROUGHPUT  
          START TOTAL_RECORDS  
          RESULT 10  
          TIME NA  
          END TOTAL_RECORDS  

          Explanation:  
          – “START & END” delimiters tell reviewers which query is being reported.  
          – “RESULT” holds the scalar value returned by the SQL.  
          – “TIME” is set to the literal string “NA” because deterministic timing is
            impossible in a test environment; using any other value will cause an
            automated failure.

    6.  Verification expectations (summary)  
        After you finish, the test harness will check that:  
        • All three directories exist.  
        • app_metrics.csv has the exact contents shown above.  
        • /home/user/perf/db/metrics.db exists, contains a table called metrics
          with ten rows, and contains the index idx_latency.  
        • analysis.log exists in /home/user/perf/output and matches the twelve lines
          verbatim, including newline placement.  

    Tips (not exhaustive):  
        – Use only user-level commands; you do not have root access.  
        – Remember you can give multiple SQLite directives non-interactively by
          passing them to sqlite3 with -cmd or via heredoc.  
        – Do not hard-code absolute paths inside SQL IMPORT statements; SQLite’s CLI
          sees the path exactly as you type it.  
        – Feel free to run intermediary checks with sqlite3 if you wish, but the
          final deliverable for grading is analysis.log with the exact contents
          listed above.
truth:
CSV_PATH=/home/user/perf/input/app_metrics.csv
    DB_PATH=/home/user/perf/db/metrics.db
    LOG_PATH=/home/user/perf/output/analysis.log

    Expected CSV content (11 lines): 
    ts,app,latency_ms,throughput_ops,memory_mb
    1,appA,120,3000,512
    2,appA,110,3200,530
    3,appB,130,3100,490
    4,appB,125,3300,500
    5,appC,100,2900,450
    6,appC,105,2950,455
    7,appA,115,3250,540
    8,appB,128,3350,505
    9,appC,102,2980,460
    10,appA,118,3280,550

    Expected database: one file metrics.db.
      Table: metrics
      Columns: ts INTEGER, app TEXT, latency_ms INTEGER, throughput_ops INTEGER, memory_mb INTEGER
      Row count: 10
      Index: idx_latency on latency_ms

    Expected results:
      AVG_LATENCY = 115.30  (TEXT stored as '115.30' by SQLite round)
      MAX_THROUGHPUT = 3350
      TOTAL_RECORDS = 10

    Expected log content (exact 12 lines, \n endings):
    START AVG_LATENCY\n
    RESULT 115.30\n
    TIME NA\n
    END AVG_LATENCY\n
    START MAX_THROUGHPUT\n
    RESULT 3350\n
    TIME NA\n
    END MAX_THROUGHPUT\n
    START TOTAL_RECORDS\n
    RESULT 10\n
    TIME NA\n
    END TOTAL_RECORDS\n
